title: The AI Bubble Is No Ordinary Bubble
excerpt: Tech companies need to generate huge revenues fast, or the economy could be in trouble.
content_html:
The American stock market is booming, thanks to artificial intelligence. Tech giants are borrowing billions to acquire AI talent, purchase chips and hardware, and construct data centers. And market watchers are starting to get worried. They see financiers bulldozing giant piles of money to private AI start-ups with no realistic path to profitability, tech companies reliant on other tech companies for revenue growth, and non-tech businesses without a lot to show for their AI investments. The value of AI-linked firms has climbed $27 trillion in the past three years—an astonishing amount, equivalent to 36 percent of the value of the entire U.S. stock market today. Although future earnings could justify those valuations, as Dominic Wilson and Vickie Chang of Goldman Sachs argued in a note to clients, the profit expectations require Panglossian optimism.

No less an authority than Sam Altman is arguing that we are in an AI bubble. The International Monetary Fund is citing it as a significant risk to financial stability and warning about what might happen when it bursts: diminished investment, tighter credit, reduced consumption, disrupted trade flows.

That’s pretty much what happens when any bubble pops, as a Dutch tulip obsessive could have told you in 1637 or a bitcoin evangelist could have told you in 2011, 2013, 2014, 2018, or 2022. Yet the AI bubble is no ordinary bubble. Hyper-rich corporations are stoking it, rather than kitchen-table investors. They’re blowing it up when credit is fairly expensive, not dirt cheap. That might make the bubble less fragile and longer lasting than those of the past. But it won’t make it any less painful when it pops.

The dot-com bubble of the late 1990s and the housing bubble of the late aughts were remarkably broad-based compared with the AI bubble today. Uncle Ted got a hulking desktop computer, opened a newfangled E-Trade account, and started day-trading shares in Apple and Pets.com. The share of American households owning equities climbed 13 percentage points from 1995 to 2001, during which time more than 2,752 firms went public (far more than the 730 operating businesses that have IPOed in the past six years). A half decade later, Aunt Linda bought a condo with nothing down, flipped it, and mortgaged three new investment properties in the Phoenix suburbs, sight unseen. The homeownership rate rose 5 percentage points as the housing bubble inflated; 40 percent of mortgages issued at its height went to investment or vacation properties.

In both cases, regular people were staking their savings on what seemed like a winning bet: the internet changing everything, housing prices never going down. In both cases, cheap credit fueled the irrational exuberance. Low interest rates let venture capitalists fund nonsense web businesses and let banks provide junk loans to borrowers with terrible credit. In both cases, rising interest rates popped the bubble.

Today, Uncle Ted and Aunt Linda aren’t really getting in on the AI frenzy. The share of Americans who own stocks has held steady. Household debt has grown, but it has fallen relative to disposable income and GDP. Everybody seems to know someone who was personally burned by the dot-com collapse and the housing crisis. How many people know someone who’s staking it all on OpenAI and Anthropic today? (The companies aren’t public, after all.) Indeed, how many people know someone whose livelihood has been directly affected by the AI frenzy at all?

The insularity of the AI bubble isn’t the only thing that makes it unusual, and we should probably think about it as two overlapping bubbles instead of just one. AI is driving tremendous spending on capital expenditures—physical infrastructure, software development. And it is driving a tremendous run-up in company valuations.

Digital innovations don’t tend to require a ton of labor or heavy equipment. The companies offering them tend to be capital-light, like marketers and insurers, rather than capital-intensive, like airlines and hotel chains. But AI is different. A start-up might need thousands of times as much computing power to train an algorithm as it would need to develop a conventional software product. To get that power, Silicon Valley is building 1,500 data centers in the United States and counting, and purchasing a mammoth quantity of semiconductor chips. (The Wall Street Journal calls these chips “the 21st century’s most important market.”) Amazon, Microsoft, Alphabet, and Meta alone are spending more than $700 billion on the build-out this year. AI-infrastructure investment is responsible for essentially all American GDP growth at the moment. Without it, in other words, we might be in a recession.

The boom is lifting the value of farmland, driving up the cost of construction employment, and flushing huge sums to water and electrical utilities. Still, tech companies are the main beneficiaries: Nvidia is selling chips to Meta; Amazon is selling cloud-computing capacity to OpenAI. This surge in revenue, and the excitement about the money to be made when AI starts bolstering productivity and profits, is driving up valuations. The Magnificent Seven—Alphabet, Amazon, Apple, Meta, Microsoft, Nvidia, and Tesla—now account for one-third of the value of the Standard & Poor’s 500. OpenAI is worth more than Eli Lilly, JPMorgan Chase, Visa, Costco, Exxon Mobil, Wells Fargo, CVS Health, McDonald’s, and Boeing.

Tech companies are going to need to start generating huge revenues and huge profits to justify these valuations. OpenAI needs to spin up roughly $100 billion in free cash flow by 2030, according to calculations by Harrison Rolfes of PitchBook. Analysts expect it will lose $10 billion to $30 billion that year. If it does—or if more communities ban data centers, or if non-tech companies prove reticent about purchasing AI software, or if China develops AI models that do not require so much computing power—we could be in for a massive correction. The AI economy is a trillion-dollar ouroboros of buying and selling, investment and equity staking, all happening between San Francisco and San Jose. Big Tech is advancing money to AI start-ups to buy cloud services from Big Tech, which is using the revenue to run new AI models … you get the idea. What happens to one firm could happen to all of them.

Tech companies are also going to need to start generating even bigger revenues and profits to pay off their debts. In the early days of AI, venture capitalists, wealthy individuals, and Big Tech firms used hard cash to invest in the new technology. But the build-out has proven so expensive that Silicon Valley has turned to corporate bonds and private credit, meaning loans made by entities other than banks. Thus, though the AI boom has happened while interest rates are fairly high, it still involves a lot of leverage. The deals are complicated, opaque, and structured so as to be invisible on traditional balance sheets, making the “ultimate distribution of risk less transparent,” Stijn Van Nieuwerburgh of Columbia Business School has found. Already, lenders are getting queasy—“buy-side indigestion,” as Morningstar describes the market’s recent reticence to meet Silicon Valley’s demands.

When the bubble bursts, Uncle Ted and Aunt Linda will be affected, even if they were never the ones stoking the frenzy. Their retirement plans and pensions are invested in Silicon Valley stocks that could drop; their small businesses are reliant on access to credit that might get throttled. But, hey, there’s always the possibility that AI might steal their job before any of that happens.

blocks.html_en: The American stock market is booming, thanks to artificial intelligence. Tech giants are borrowing billions to acquire AI talent, purchase chips and hardware, and construct data centers. And market watchers are starting to get worried. They see financiers bulldozing giant piles of money to private AI start-ups with no realistic path to profitability, tech companies reliant on other tech companies for revenue growth, and non-tech businesses without a lot to show for their AI investments. The value of AI-linked firms has climbed $27 trillion in the past three years—an astonishing amount, equivalent to 36 percent of the value of the entire U.S. stock market today. Although future earnings could justify those valuations, as Dominic Wilson and Vickie Chang of Goldman Sachs argued in a note to clients, the profit expectations require Panglossian optimism.

sentences.en: The American stock market is booming, thanks to artificial intelligence.
sentences.zh: 得益于人工智能，美国股市正在蓬勃发展。

sentences.en: Tech giants are borrowing billions to acquire AI talent, purchase chips and hardware, and construct data centers.
sentences.zh: 科技巨头正在借贷数十亿美元来收购人工智能人才、购买芯片和硬件以及建设数据中心。

sentences.en: And market watchers are starting to get worried.
sentences.zh: 市场观察人士开始感到担忧。

sentences.en: They see financiers bulldozing giant piles of money to private AI start-ups with no realistic path to profitability, tech companies reliant on other tech companies for revenue growth, and non-tech businesses without a lot to show for their AI investments.
sentences.zh: 他们看到金融家将巨额资金投入到没有现实盈利途径的私营人工智能初创企业、依赖其他科技公司实现收入增长的科技公司以及没有太多人工智能投资成果的非科技企业。

sentences.en: The value of AI-linked firms has climbed $27 trillion in the past three years—an astonishing amount, equivalent to 36 percent of the value of the entire U.S. stock market today.
sentences.zh: 过去三年，人工智能相关企业的价值攀升了 27 万亿美元，这一数字令人震惊，相当于当今整个美国股市价值的 36%。

sentences.en: Although future earnings could justify those valuations, as Dominic Wilson and Vickie Chang of Goldman Sachs argued in a note to clients, the profit expectations require Panglossian optimism.
sentences.zh: 正如高盛的 Dominic Wilson 和 Vickie Chang 在给客户的一份报告中指出的那样，尽管未来的盈利可能证明这些估值是合理的，但利润预期需要乐观的态度。

blocks.html_en: No less an authority than Sam Altman is arguing that we are in an AI bubble. The International Monetary Fund is citing it as a significant risk to financial stability and warning about what might happen when it bursts: diminished investment, tighter credit, reduced consumption, disrupted trade flows.

sentences.en: No less an authority than Sam Altman is arguing that we are in an AI bubble.
sentences.zh: 萨姆·奥尔特曼 (Sam Altman) 等权威人士认为我们正处于人工智能泡沫之中。

sentences.en: The International Monetary Fund is citing it as a significant risk to financial stability and warning about what might happen when it bursts: diminished investment, tighter credit, reduced consumption, disrupted trade flows.
sentences.zh: 国际货币基金组织将其视为金融稳定的重大风险，并警告其爆发时可能发生的情况：投资减少、信贷收紧、消费减少、贸易流动中断。

blocks.html_en: That’s pretty much what happens when any bubble pops, as a Dutch tulip obsessive could have told you in 1637 or a bitcoin evangelist could have told you in 2011, 2013, 2014, 2018, or 2022. Yet the AI bubble is no ordinary bubble. Hyper-rich corporations are stoking it, rather than kitchen-table investors. They’re blowing it up when credit is fairly expensive, not dirt cheap. That might make the bubble less fragile and longer lasting than those of the past. But it won’t make it any less painful when it pops.

sentences.en: That’s pretty much what happens when any bubble pops, as a Dutch tulip obsessive could have told you in 1637 or a bitcoin evangelist could have told you in 2011, 2013, 2014, 2018, or 2022.
sentences.zh: 这几乎就是任何泡沫破灭时都会发生的情况，正如一位荷兰郁金香迷可能在 1637 年告诉你的那样，或者比特币传播者可能在 2011 年、2013 年、2014 年、2018 年或 2022 年告诉你的那样。

sentences.en: Yet the AI bubble is no ordinary bubble.
sentences.zh: 然而，人工智能泡沫并不是普通的泡沫。

sentences.en: Hyper-rich corporations are stoking it, rather than kitchen-table investors.
sentences.zh: 火上浇油的是超级富有的企业，而不是餐桌上的投资者。

sentences.en: They’re blowing it up when credit is fairly expensive, not dirt cheap.
sentences.zh: 当信贷相当昂贵而不是非常便宜时，他们就会把它炸毁。

sentences.en: That might make the bubble less fragile and longer lasting than those of the past.
sentences.zh: 与过去相比，这可能会让泡沫变得不那么脆弱、更持久。

sentences.en: But it won’t make it any less painful when it pops.
sentences.zh: 但当它弹出时，它并不会减轻疼痛。

sentences.en: The dot-com bubble of the late 1990s and the housing bubble of the late aughts were remarkably broad-based compared with the AI bubble today.
sentences.zh: 与今天的人工智能泡沫相比，20 世纪 90 年代末的互联网泡沫和 20 世纪 90 年代末的房地产泡沫的基础非常广泛。

sentences.en: Uncle Ted got a hulking desktop computer, opened a newfangled E-Trade account, and started day-trading shares in Apple and Pets.com.
sentences.zh: 特德叔叔买了一台笨重的台式电脑，开设了一个新奇的电子交易账户，并开始在苹果和 Pets.com 上进行日内交易。

sentences.en: The share of American households owning equities climbed 13 percentage points from 1995 to 2001, during which time more than 2,752 firms went public (far more than the 730 operating businesses that have IPOed in the past six years).
sentences.zh: 从 1995 年到 2001 年，拥有股票的美国家庭比例上升了 13 个百分点，在此期间，超过 2,752 家公司上市（远远多于过去六年中 IPO 的 730 家运营企业）。

sentences.en: A half decade later, Aunt Linda bought a condo with nothing down, flipped it, and mortgaged three new investment properties in the Phoenix suburbs, sight unseen.
sentences.zh: 五年后，琳达阿姨买了一套公寓，没有首付，将其翻转，并抵押了凤凰城郊区的三处新投资房产，但从未见过面。

sentences.en: The homeownership rate rose 5 percentage points as the housing bubble inflated; 40 percent of mortgages issued at its height went to investment or vacation properties.
sentences.zh: 随着房地产泡沫的膨胀，住房拥有率上升了5个百分点；在高峰时期发放的抵押贷款中有 40% 用于投资或度假房产。

In both cases, cheap credit fueled the irrational exuberance. Low interest rates let venture capitalists fund nonsense web businesses and let banks provide junk loans to borrowers with terrible credit. In both cases, rising interest rates popped the bubble.

sentences.en: In both cases, regular people were staking their savings on what seemed like a winning bet: the internet changing everything, housing prices never going down.
sentences.zh: 在这两种情况下，普通人都把自己的积蓄押在了看似胜利的赌注上：互联网改变了一切，房价永远不会下降。

sentences.en: In both cases, cheap credit fueled the irrational exuberance.
sentences.zh: 在这两种情况下，廉价信贷都助长了非理性繁荣。

sentences.en: Low interest rates let venture capitalists fund nonsense web businesses and let banks provide junk loans to borrowers with terrible credit.
sentences.zh: 低利率让风险资本家为无意义的网络企业提供资金，并让银行向信用不佳的借款人提供垃圾贷款。

sentences.en: In both cases, rising interest rates popped the bubble.
sentences.zh: 在这两种情况下，利率上升都戳破了泡沫。

Indeed, how many people know someone whose livelihood has been directly affected by the AI frenzy at all?

sentences.zh: 如今，特德叔叔和琳达阿姨并没有真正参与人工智能的热潮。

sentences.en: The share of Americans who own stocks has held steady.
sentences.zh: 持有股票的美国人比例保持稳定。

sentences.en: Household debt has grown, but it has fallen relative to disposable income and GDP.
sentences.zh: 家庭债务有所增加，但相对于可支配收入和国内生产总值而言有所下降。

sentences.en: Everybody seems to know someone who was personally burned by the dot-com collapse and the housing crisis.
sentences.zh: 每个人似乎都认识一些因互联网泡沫破裂和住房危机而遭受过损失的人。

sentences.en: How many people know someone who’s staking it all on OpenAI and Anthropic today?
sentences.zh: 如今有多少人认识将全部赌注押在 OpenAI 和 Anthropic 上的人？

sentences.en: (The companies aren’t public, after all.)
sentences.zh: （毕竟这些公司不是上市公司。）

sentences.en: Indeed, how many people know someone whose livelihood has been directly affected by the AI frenzy at all?
sentences.zh: 事实上，有多少人知道有人的生计直接受到人工智能热潮的影响？

sentences.zh: 人工智能正在推动巨大的资本支出——物理基础设施、软件开发。

sentences.en: And it is driving a tremendous run-up in company valuations.
sentences.zh: 它正在推动公司估值大幅上涨。

sentences.zh: 数字创新往往不需要大量劳动力或重型设备。

sentences.en: The companies offering them tend to be capital-light, like marketers and insurers, rather than capital-intensive, like airlines and hotel chains.
sentences.zh: 提供这些服务的公司往往是轻资本的，如营销商和保险公司，而不是资本密集型的​​，如航空公司和连锁酒店。

sentences.en: But AI is different.
sentences.zh: 但人工智能不同。

sentences.en: To get that power, Silicon Valley is building 1,500 data centers in the United States and counting, and purchasing a mammoth quantity of semiconductor chips.
sentences.zh: 为了获得这种能力，硅谷正在美国建设 1,500 个数据中心，并且还在计算和购买大量的半导体芯片。

sentences.en: (The Wall Street Journal calls these chips “the 21st century’s most important market.”) Amazon, Microsoft, Alphabet, and Meta alone are spending more than $700 billion on the build-out this year.
sentences.zh: （《华尔街日报》称这些芯片为“21 世纪最重要的市场”。）今年，仅亚马逊、微软、Alphabet 和 Meta 就在扩建上花费了超过 7000 亿美元。

sentences.en: AI-infrastructure investment is responsible for essentially all American GDP growth at the moment.
sentences.zh: 目前美国 GDP 增长基本上全部来自人工智能基础设施投资。

sentences.en: Without it, in other words, we might be in a recession.
sentences.zh: 换句话说，如果没有它，我们可能会陷入衰退。

blocks.html_en: The boom is lifting the value of farmland, driving up the cost of construction employment, and flushing huge sums to water and electrical utilities. Still, tech companies are the main beneficiaries: Nvidia is selling chips to Meta; Amazon is selling cloud-computing capacity to OpenAI. This surge in revenue, and the excitement about the money to be made when AI starts bolstering productivity and profits, is driving up valuations. The Magnificent Seven—Alphabet, Amazon, Apple, Meta, Microsoft, Nvidia, and Tesla—now account for one-third of the value of the Standard & Poor’s 500. OpenAI is worth more than Eli Lilly, JPMorgan Chase, Visa, Costco, Exxon Mobil, Wells Fargo, CVS Health, McDonald’s, and Boeing.

sentences.en: The boom is lifting the value of farmland, driving up the cost of construction employment, and flushing huge sums to water and electrical utilities.
sentences.zh: 繁荣正在提升农田的价值，推高建筑业就业成本，并为水电设施带来巨额资金。

sentences.en: Still, tech companies are the main beneficiaries: Nvidia is selling chips to Meta; Amazon is selling cloud-computing capacity to OpenAI.
sentences.zh: 尽管如此，科技公司仍然是主要受益者：Nvidia 正在向 Meta 出售芯片；亚马逊正在向 OpenAI 出售云计算能力。

sentences.en: This surge in revenue, and the excitement about the money to be made when AI starts bolstering productivity and profits, is driving up valuations.
sentences.zh: 收入的激增，以及当人工智能开始提高生产力和利润时对赚钱的兴奋，正在推高估值。

sentences.en: The Magnificent Seven—Alphabet, Amazon, Apple, Meta, Microsoft, Nvidia, and Tesla—now account for one-third of the value of the Standard & Poor’s 500.
sentences.zh: 七巨头——Alphabet、亚马逊、苹果、Meta、微软、英伟达和特斯拉——目前占标准普尔 500 指数市值的三分之一。

sentences.en: OpenAI is worth more than Eli Lilly, JPMorgan Chase, Visa, Costco, Exxon Mobil, Wells Fargo, CVS Health, McDonald’s, and Boeing.
sentences.zh: OpenAI 的价值超过礼来公司、摩根大通、Visa、好市多、埃克森美孚、富国银行、CVS Health、麦当劳和波音。

blocks.html_en: Tech companies are going to need to start generating huge revenues and huge profits to justify these valuations. OpenAI needs to spin up roughly $100 billion in free cash flow by 2030, according to calculations by Harrison Rolfes of PitchBook. Analysts expect it will lose $10 billion to $30 billion that year. If it does—or if more communities ban data centers, or if non-tech companies prove reticent about purchasing AI software, or if China develops AI models that do not require so much computing power—we could be in for a massive correction. The AI economy is a trillion-dollar ouroboros of buying and selling, investment and equity staking, all happening between San Francisco and San Jose. Big Tech is advancing money to AI start-ups to buy cloud services from Big Tech, which is using the revenue to run new AI models … you get the idea. What happens to one firm could happen to all of them.

sentences.en: Tech companies are going to need to start generating huge revenues and huge profits to justify these valuations.
sentences.zh: 科技公司将需要开始产生巨额收入和巨额利润来证明这些估值的合理性。

sentences.en: OpenAI needs to spin up roughly $100 billion in free cash flow by 2030, according to calculations by Harrison Rolfes of PitchBook.
sentences.zh: 根据 PitchBook 的 Harrison Rolfes 的计算，到 2030 年，OpenAI 需要产生大约 1000 亿美元的自由现金流。

sentences.en: Analysts expect it will lose $10 billion to $30 billion that year.
sentences.zh: 分析师预计该公司当年将损失 100 亿至 300 亿美元。

sentences.en: If it does—or if more communities ban data centers, or if non-tech companies prove reticent about purchasing AI software, or if China develops AI models that do not require so much computing power—we could be in for a massive correction.
sentences.zh: 如果确实如此——或者如果更多的社区禁止数据中心，或者如果非科技公司不愿购买人工智能软件，或者如果中国开发出不需要如此多计算能力的人工智能模型——我们可能会面临大规模的修正。

sentences.en: The AI economy is a trillion-dollar ouroboros of buying and selling, investment and equity staking, all happening between San Francisco and San Jose.
sentences.zh: 人工智能经济是一个万亿美元的买卖、投资和股权投资的衔尾蛇，所有这些都发生在旧金山和圣何塞之间。

sentences.en: Big Tech is advancing money to AI start-ups to buy cloud services from Big Tech, which is using the revenue to run new AI models …
sentences.zh: 大型科技公司正在向人工智能初创企业提供资金，以从大型科技公司购买云服务，而大型科技公司则利用这些收入来运行新的人工智能模型……

sentences.en: you get the idea.
sentences.zh: 你明白了。

sentences.en: What happens to one firm could happen to all of them.
sentences.zh: 一家公司发生的事情也可能发生在所有公司身上。

blocks.html_en: Tech companies are also going to need to start generating even bigger revenues and profits to pay off their debts. In the early days of AI, venture capitalists, wealthy individuals, and Big Tech firms used hard cash to invest in the new technology. But the build-out has proven so expensive that Silicon Valley has turned to corporate bonds and private credit, meaning loans made by entities other than banks. Thus, though the AI boom has happened while interest rates are fairly high, it still involves a lot of leverage. The deals are complicated, opaque, and structured so as to be invisible on traditional balance sheets, making the “ultimate distribution of risk less transparent,” Stijn Van Nieuwerburgh of Columbia Business School has found. Already, lenders are getting queasy—“buy-side indigestion,” as Morningstar describes the market’s recent reticence to meet Silicon Valley’s demands.

sentences.en: Tech companies are also going to need to start generating even bigger revenues and profits to pay off their debts.
sentences.zh: 科技公司还需要开始产生更大的收入和利润来偿还债务。

sentences.en: In the early days of AI, venture capitalists, wealthy individuals, and Big Tech firms used hard cash to invest in the new technology.
sentences.zh: 在人工智能的早期，风险投资家、富有的个人和大型科技公司使用现金来投资新技术。

sentences.en: But the build-out has proven so expensive that Silicon Valley has turned to corporate bonds and private credit, meaning loans made by entities other than banks.
sentences.zh: 但事实证明，扩建成本如此之高，以至于硅谷转向了公司债券和私人信贷，即银行以外的实体提供的贷款。

sentences.en: Thus, though the AI boom has happened while interest rates are fairly high, it still involves a lot of leverage.
sentences.zh: 因此，尽管人工智能热潮是在利率相当高的情况下发生的，但它仍然涉及大量杠杆。

sentences.en: The deals are complicated, opaque, and structured so as to be invisible on traditional balance sheets, making the “ultimate distribution of risk less transparent,” Stijn Van Nieuwerburgh of Columbia Business School has found.
sentences.zh: 哥伦比亚商学院的 Stijn Van Nieuwerburgh 发现，这些交易复杂、不透明，结构复杂，在传统资产负债表上不可见，导致“风险的最终分配不太透明”。

sentences.en: Already, lenders are getting queasy—“buy-side indigestion,” as Morningstar describes the market’s recent reticence to meet Silicon Valley’s demands.
sentences.zh: 贷款机构已经开始感到不安——正如晨星公司描述的那样，市场最近对满足硅谷的要求保持沉默，这是“买方消化不良”。

blocks.html_en: When the bubble bursts, Uncle Ted and Aunt Linda will be affected, even if they were never the ones stoking the frenzy. Their retirement plans and pensions are invested in Silicon Valley stocks that could drop; their small businesses are reliant on access to credit that might get throttled. But, hey, there’s always the possibility that AI might steal their job before any of that happens.

sentences.en: When the bubble bursts, Uncle Ted and Aunt Linda will be affected, even if they were never the ones stoking the frenzy.
sentences.zh: 当泡沫破裂时，特德叔叔和琳达阿姨将受到影响，即使他们从来都不是煽动狂热的人。

sentences.en: Their retirement plans and pensions are invested in Silicon Valley stocks that could drop; their small businesses are reliant on access to credit that might get throttled.
sentences.zh: 他们的退休计划和养老金投资于可能下跌的硅谷股票；他们的小企业依赖信贷，而信贷可能会受到限制。

sentences.en: But, hey, there’s always the possibility that AI might steal their job before any of that happens.
sentences.zh: 但是，嘿，人工智能总是有可能在这一切发生之前抢走他们的工作。

content_html_zh:
得益于人工智能，美国股市正在蓬勃发展。 科技巨头正在借贷数十亿美元来收购人工智能人才、购买芯片和硬件以及建设数据中心。 市场观察人士开始感到担忧。 他们看到金融家将巨额资金投入到没有现实盈利途径的私营人工智能初创企业、依赖其他科技公司实现收入增长的科技公司以及没有太多人工智能投资成果的非科技企业。 过去三年，人工智能相关企业的价值攀升了 27 万亿美元，这一数字令人震惊，相当于当今整个美国股市价值的 36%。 正如高盛的 Dominic Wilson 和 Vickie Chang 在给客户的一份报告中指出的那样，尽管未来的盈利可能证明这些估值是合理的，但利润预期需要乐观的态度。

萨姆·奥尔特曼 (Sam Altman) 等权威人士认为我们正处于人工智能泡沫之中。 国际货币基金组织将其视为金融稳定的重大风险，并警告其爆发时可能发生的情况：投资减少、信贷收紧、消费减少、贸易流动中断。

这几乎就是任何泡沫破灭时都会发生的情况，正如一位荷兰郁金香迷可能在 1637 年告诉你的那样，或者比特币传播者可能在 2011 年、2013 年、2014 年、2018 年或 2022 年告诉你的那样。 然而，人工智能泡沫并不是普通的泡沫。 火上浇油的是超级富有的企业，而不是餐桌上的投资者。 当信贷相当昂贵而不是非常便宜时，他们就会把它炸毁。 与过去相比，这可能会让泡沫变得不那么脆弱、更持久。 但当它弹出时，它并不会减轻疼痛。

与今天的人工智能泡沫相比，20 世纪 90 年代末的互联网泡沫和 20 世纪 90 年代末的房地产泡沫的基础非常广泛。 特德叔叔买了一台笨重的台式电脑，开设了一个新奇的电子交易账户，并开始在苹果和 Pets.com 上进行日内交易。 从 1995 年到 2001 年，拥有股票的美国家庭比例上升了 13 个百分点，在此期间，超过 2,752 家公司上市（远远多于过去六年中 IPO 的 730 家运营企业）。 五年后，琳达阿姨买了一套公寓，没有首付，将其翻转，并抵押了凤凰城郊区的三处新投资房产，但从未见过面。 随着房地产泡沫的膨胀，住房拥有率上升了5个百分点；在高峰时期发放的抵押贷款中有 40% 用于投资或度假房产。

在这两种情况下，普通人都把自己的积蓄押在了看似胜利的赌注上：互联网改变了一切，房价永远不会下降。 在这两种情况下，廉价信贷都助长了非理性繁荣。 低利率让风险资本家为无意义的网络企业提供资金，并让银行向信用不佳的借款人提供垃圾贷款。 在这两种情况下，利率上升都戳破了泡沫。

如今，特德叔叔和琳达阿姨并没有真正参与人工智能的热潮。 持有股票的美国人比例保持稳定。 家庭债务有所增加，但相对于可支配收入和国内生产总值而言有所下降。 每个人似乎都认识一些因互联网泡沫破裂和住房危机而遭受过损失的人。 如今有多少人认识将全部赌注押在 OpenAI 和 Anthropic 上的人？ （毕竟这些公司不是上市公司。） 事实上，有多少人知道有人的生计直接受到人工智能热潮的影响？

人工智能泡沫的孤立性并不是使其不同寻常的唯一原因，我们或许应该将其视为两个重叠的泡沫，而不是一个。 人工智能正在推动巨大的资本支出——物理基础设施、软件开发。 它正在推动公司估值大幅上涨。

数字创新往往不需要大量劳动力或重型设备。 提供这些服务的公司往往是轻资本的，如营销商和保险公司，而不是资本密集型的​​，如航空公司和连锁酒店。 但人工智能不同。 初创公司训练算法所需的计算能力可能是开发传统软件产品所需的数千倍。 为了获得这种能力，硅谷正在美国建设 1,500 个数据中心，并且还在计算和购买大量的半导体芯片。 （《华尔街日报》称这些芯片为“21 世纪最重要的市场”。）今年，仅亚马逊、微软、Alphabet 和 Meta 就在扩建上花费了超过 7000 亿美元。 目前美国 GDP 增长基本上全部来自人工智能基础设施投资。 换句话说，如果没有它，我们可能会陷入衰退。

繁荣正在提升农田的价值，推高建筑业就业成本，并为水电设施带来巨额资金。 尽管如此，科技公司仍然是主要受益者：Nvidia 正在向 Meta 出售芯片；亚马逊正在向 OpenAI 出售云计算能力。 收入的激增，以及当人工智能开始提高生产力和利润时对赚钱的兴奋，正在推高估值。 七巨头——Alphabet、亚马逊、苹果、Meta、微软、英伟达和特斯拉——目前占标准普尔 500 指数市值的三分之一。 OpenAI 的价值超过礼来公司、摩根大通、Visa、好市多、埃克森美孚、富国银行、CVS Health、麦当劳和波音。

科技公司将需要开始产生巨额收入和巨额利润来证明这些估值的合理性。 根据 PitchBook 的 Harrison Rolfes 的计算，到 2030 年，OpenAI 需要产生大约 1000 亿美元的自由现金流。 分析师预计该公司当年将损失 100 亿至 300 亿美元。 如果确实如此——或者如果更多的社区禁止数据中心，或者如果非科技公司不愿购买人工智能软件，或者如果中国开发出不需要如此多计算能力的人工智能模型——我们可能会面临大规模的修正。 人工智能经济是一个万亿美元的买卖、投资和股权投资的衔尾蛇，所有这些都发生在旧金山和圣何塞之间。 大型科技公司正在向人工智能初创企业提供资金，以从大型科技公司购买云服务，而大型科技公司则利用这些收入来运行新的人工智能模型…… 你明白了。 一家公司发生的事情也可能发生在所有公司身上。

科技公司还需要开始产生更大的收入和利润来偿还债务。 在人工智能的早期，风险投资家、富有的个人和大型科技公司使用现金来投资新技术。 但事实证明，扩建成本如此之高，以至于硅谷转向了公司债券和私人信贷，即银行以外的实体提供的贷款。 因此，尽管人工智能热潮是在利率相当高的情况下发生的，但它仍然涉及大量杠杆。 哥伦比亚商学院的 Stijn Van Nieuwerburgh 发现，这些交易复杂、不透明，结构复杂，在传统资产负债表上不可见，导致“风险的最终分配不太透明”。 贷款机构已经开始感到不安——正如晨星公司描述的那样，市场最近对满足硅谷的要求保持沉默，这是“买方消化不良”。

当泡沫破裂时，特德叔叔和琳达阿姨将受到影响，即使他们从来都不是煽动狂热的人。 他们的退休计划和养老金投资于可能下跌的硅谷股票；他们的小企业依赖信贷，而信贷可能会受到限制。 但是，嘿，人工智能总是有可能在这一切发生之前抢走他们的工作。
